availability.p: Periodicals and books published before 1 January 1923 are in the public domain. If re-publishing, quoting from, or reproducing images from this digital archive, we request that you cite it as follows: Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive, Center for Digital Humanities, University of South Carolina.

Periodicals and books published on or after 1 January 1923 may be protected by U.S. Copyright Law. All attempts to secure permission for republishing post-1923 material in this archive have been made. The names, logos, and identifying images, as well as the content of the articles, remain the property of the periodicals and/or of the authors and artists themselves. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder. Archival material published in this archive is used with permission of the institution or library that holds it. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder.
Responsibility for any use of the contents of this digital archive rests exclusively with the user.

bibl.title: The Lost Woman of San Nicolas

rs.persName: THE LOST WOMAN OF SAN NICOLAS

div.p: San Nicolas Island is not classified as one of the Channel Islands, but it belongs to what navigators term Cortez' Shoal. The islands in the Channel group are San Miguel, Santa Rosa, Santa Cruz and Anacapa. Those in Cortez' Shoal group are St. Catalina, San Clemente and San Nicolas, and also included is what is known as John Bogg's Rock, seven miles northward from the northern extremity of San Nicolas, also Santa Barbara Island, a mere rock midway between Catalina and San Nicolas.

It is with San Nicolas that this article has to deal and because of the wonderful story of the Indian woman who was left alone on the island in the [eighteen-]thirties and who subsisted there for 18 years following. San Nicolas lies 80 miles southward from Ventura and the mainland and nearly as many miles from any other land of presentable size, the nearest being the island of Santa Catalina, 60 miles away. In dimension it is about one-third the size of Santa Catalina. It has springs of water on it, is reasonably well watered by winter rains and will support several thousand head of sheep. It is wind-swept and at times large sections of it are covered with shifting sands. It is about nine miles in diameter.

There has been much written of the Indian woman who was left on the island and much, of course, that was not true. According to the best authorities the island in its earlier days sustained a large Indian population. The story begins with the removal of a number of Indian women from the island in 1836. According to John Nidever, who was an uncle of the late Jacob Nidever of Ventura County and others who hunted otter about the island as early as 1835, the Alaska Indians were in the habit of making periodic visits to the islands for otter and other skins, with their Russian masters.

p.span: They were savage people, the Alaskan Kadiacs, and made fierce attacks against all who attempted otter hunting on any of the islands. They were supplied with firearms and were dangerous foes even to the white men who were as well armed, and much more so to the natives, who had only bows and arrows and stone implements of warfare. In 1836 a party of these Indians who were left on the islands by a Russian vessel, chased Nidever and his party to their landing, and were only repulsed by a sharp fire which killed several of their men. The chase was on the water in boats, and the contest was in trying to prevent them landing at the only practicable place.

div.p: According to the best authority, a party of these Indians took possession of San Nicolas Island for the purpose of hunting otter, and finally took possession of the women, and slew every man and male child on the island, in the massacre that ensued. When the Alaskans abandoned San Nicolas, after the hunt was over, they left the women to their fate. It was some years subsequent to this that the padres employed men on the mainland to go to the island and remove the survivors.

Recent investigations made of the island and those things left by the residents of the islands shows that the former inhabitants were of the Toltec or Aztec races, and hence, it is said came the white skin and pleasant manner of the rescued Indian woman. The following is the story as told in an early day by Nidever, the man who finally rescued the woman:

head.title: Story of the Rescue By Nidever.

div.p: I arrived on the coast in 1834, in the month of November. In the early part of the following year, 1835, I came to Santa Barbara and engaged in otter hunting, which I have followed almost uninterrupted until within a few years. (This statement was made in the early [eighteen-]seventies.) At the beginning of 1835 Isaac J. Sparks and Lewis T. Burton, Americans, also otter hunters, settled here and chartered the schooner "Peores-Nada," (worse than nothing), for the trip to the lower California coast. The schooner was commanded by Chas. Hubbard, who was hired by the owner of the schooner, a Spaniard at Monterey. The crew placed in her by Sparks and Burton was, with two or three exceptions composed of Kanakas, natives of Hawaii. The "Peores-Nada left Santa Barbara the latter part of April, 1835. About three months after she returned to San Pedro and from there went directly to the island of San Nicolas for the purpose of taking off the Indians then living there. Sparks, who hunted with me several years afterwards, told me the story, but I cannot now recollect who authorized or caused their removal. I remember distinctly, however, that a man by the name of Williams, a former acquaintance of mine in the Rocky Mountains, took an interest and was one of the party that assisted in the removal. Also a man from Los Angeles was of the party. The circumstance of leaving the woman alone on the island was, as near as I can recollect, from what Sparks told me, as follows:

Having got all the Indians together on the beach and ready for embarking, one of them made signs that her child had been left behind, whereupon she was allowed to go back and fetch it. She was gone some time, when a strong wind springing up, they did not [dare to wait] longer for her, fearing for the safety of the schooner.

The water, which is quite shoal about the island, becomes exceedingly rough in a storm, and there is no harbor of any kind that would afford shelter in a heavy gale. They ran before the wind and reached San Pedro in safety. Here the Indians were put ashore, some being taken to Los Angeles and some to the Mission of San Gabriel. It was the intention of the captain of the schooner to return for the woman who had been left on the island, as soon as possible. From San Pedro the "Peores-Nada came direct to Santa Barbara, took Sparks and myself over to Santa Rosa Island, and then sailed for Monterey, where she had been ordered to take a cargo of lumber to San Francisco. At the entrance to the Golden Gate the "Peores-Nada capsized and her crew were washed ashore. It was afterwards reported that the schooner drifted out to sea and was picked up by a Russian vessel, though this report was never confirmed. There were now no craft of any kind larger than the Indian canoe, and the boats of the otter hunters, left on the coast, in the southern waters, and none cared to attempt the passage of the channel to San Nicolas in an open boat. It was soon known throughout the coast that an Indian woman was left on the island. But so far as I can learn no attempt was ever made to rescue her. As the years passed by all thought that she had perished.

p.span: In 1851 I had occasion to visit San Nicolas Island. I found signs that led me to believe that the woman still survived, or that a human being was living upon the island. I had with me a man named Tom Jeffries. He and I, with one of the Indian crew landed near the lower end of the island and walked along the beach, and on the bank, close to the beach, for a distance of five or six miles. Soon after starting out we found the footprints of a human being, that, in all probability, had been made during the previous rainy season. They were distinctly defined, and for their size we concluded that they were those of a woman. We also discovered three small circular enclosures, about 200 yards back from the beach, something like a mile apart and situated on slightly rising ground. They were circular in shape, six of [or] seven feet in diameter, with walls perhaps five or six feet high, made of brush. Near these huts, or enclosures, there were stakes of driftwood stuck in the ground, and suspended upon them, at a height of five or six feet, were pieces of dried blubber, which had the appearance of having been placed there within a month or two, as they were still in a good state of preservation. With these exceptions there was nothing about the enclosures, or wind-breaks, as I would call them, that indicated that they had been occupied for years. We had come ashore early in the morning, and after finding the footprints and wind-breaks, we intended to make further search, but before noon a strong wind sprung up, and we hastened back to the schooner. We were hardly on board when the wind increased to a gale and continued to blow for about eight days, so strong at times that we expected to be blown out to sea. We were on the south side of the island, or under its lee, and in a measure protected from the wind, but the sea was so rough that we found it almost impossible to remain at anchor. Once our anchor dragged and we were compelled [to] improvise a second by filling a bag with stones. The eighth day, the wind having gone down, we were enabled to leave the island.

In the winter of 1852 I made a second trip to the island for otter, having seen large number of otter on my previous trip. On this trip I was accompanied by Charles Brown. We landed, as on our former visit, at the lower end of the island. We took two Indians ashore with us and left them in charge of the boat, while Brown and I walked along the beach, towards the head of the island. We went partly to see where the otter lay, and partly to see if we could find any sign of the Indian woman, as Father Gonzales, to whom we had reported the discoveries made on our former visit, assured us that there was no longer any doubt of her being alive. We had decided to go to the head of the island, as, for various reasons, we concluded that if she was alive she would be most likely to be found there. The water is better and more abundant there, and it is a better place for both fish and seal. We visited the huts that Jeffries had discovered and found them and their surroundings unchanged, except that it seemed to me that the seal blubber, which I had seen on my former visit, had been removed and fresh blubber hung in its place.

In the neighborhood of the huts near the shore we saw seven or eight wild dogs. They were about the size and form of a coyote, of a black and white color. I have seen the same kind of dogs among the northwest Indians. They were very wild and ran off as soon as they saw us. When within about half a mile of the head of the island, we saw a low, sandy flat that extended from one side of the island to the other. Here, we thought, in all probability, she must be living, as the ground to the north and east of this flat was high and exposed to the wind. After searching around for some time and finding no sign of her, we were about to return, having concluded that the dogs must have eaten her, as not even her bones were to be found, when I discovered in the crotch of a bush or small tree, a basket, and upon throwing off the piece of seal skin that covered it, we found within, carefully laid together a dress made of shag skins cut in square pieces, a rope made of sinew, and several small articles, such as abalone fish-hooks, bone needles, etc.

After examining them Brown proposed replacing them and returning the basket to the tree where we found it, but I scattered the contents upon the ground, telling him that if upon our return we should find them replaced in the basket it would be positive proof of the woman's existence. As it was not [now] quite late we returned to the schooner, intending to renew the search at the first opportunity, as the extreme head of the island was still unexplored by us. For the next few days, however, we were busy hunting otter, and about the fourth or fifth day a southeast wind began to blow, which soon increased to a gale. We waited about six or seven days for it to go down and then, with some difficulty, we ran over to the San Miguel Island.

I next fitted out a hunt on San Nicolas in July, 1853. My crew consisted of Charles Brown, one Irishman and four Mission Indians. This time I went with the intention of making a thorough search for the missing woman. We arrived off the island in the early part of the day and anchored opposite the middle of the southeast side about 10 a. m. Brown and I went on shore to see where the otter lay and to select a suitable camping place. We left two of our Indians in charge of the boat. We then kept along the shore without finding any signs of the Indian woman, until we reached the head of the island. Here I sat down to rest while Brown went around the head of the island and down some distance on the other side. When he returned he told me that he had seen fresh tracks of the Indian woman and had followed them from the beach up over the bank, but on the side of the ridge that formed the head of the island he had lost them, the ground being covered with moss. I was at first disposed to think that our men from the schooner had gone over there, but a moment's reflection convinced me that it would be impossible for them to get in advance of us, and besides, Brown said the tracks were too small to have been made by either of our men.
